data.title: Legacy Housing Corporation: Future Growth Prospects Are Compelling
data.description:
The last few months have been downright brutal for shareholders of Legacy Housing Corporation (LEGH). This is a fascinating company, with a focus on the production and sale of affordable tiny and manufactured homes. This is a market that I view in a favorable light, even during these tough times. You would think that, as some of the lowest-end housing opportunities available, during times of inflationary pain and persistently high-interest rates, these types of homes would see a big decline in demand. But at the end of the day, people need housing. And all this does, at least in my opinion, is push people who would normally buy traditional homes down the ladder to purchase these homes. So unless economic conditions get horrible, I would expect this kind of business to perform well.

It was with this line of thinking, as well as its relatively decent valuation, that I reaffirmed my "Buy" rating on the stock in early September of this year. Leading up to that point, the stock had been up significantly more than the broader market. But since then, something has changed. Revenue, profits, and cash flows have taken a hit. Although the average net revenue per unit has increased, the number of units sold by the company declined. This is even though the company continues to focus on some key growth opportunities. And honestly, it does weaken my previous thesis regarding demand for units in this space. Even though the stock has fallen 28% since then, which has been far worse than the 4.8% rise that the S&P 500 saw, there are reasons to be optimistic. Even with this recent weakness, shares of the company are cheap, and it boasts a net cash position as opposed to a net debt position. If anything, I do view this decline that the stock experienced as a buying opportunity.

Still A Good Home For Your Money

The only new data that we currently have regarding Legacy Housing covers through the third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, investors did see some pain. Revenue came in at $40.5 million, which was down from the $44.3 million the company reported a year earlier. This drop came even though the average price of a unit delivered rose from $63,500 to $68,500. If these prices seem low, that's because they are. Historically speaking, tiny homes and manufactured homes are incredibly cheap to build. They also tend to be smaller. Admittedly, some of the homes that Legacy Housing sells are up to 2,777 square feet. But many of them are as small as 395 square feet.

The problem for the company ended up being a decline in the number of homes sold. The number of units dropped from 475 to 420. On the pricing side, management benefited from a larger share of company-owned retail units being sold. Price hikes caused by tariffs and inflationary pressures also played a role here. Unfortunately, however, the lower end of the spectrum proved to be problematic for the company. This involved mobile Home Park demand, which dropped. Park operators are buying fewer new units because home, setup, and utility hookup costs are up significantly. But because of pressures on these low-end buyers, low rents have not been able to adjust higher by as much. It also didn't help that fewer units were being sold to independent dealers. And this is because a lot of those dealers are, in management's own words, "aging out." Naturally, the higher pricing turned out to be problematic. And management said that the average increase in the cost per home associated solely with tariffs amounted to roughly $1,200.

The decline in revenue for the company brought with it a decline in profits as well. Net income plummeted from $15.8 million to $8.6 million. Operating cash flow dropped from $13.8 million to $7.1 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $12.2 million to $9.7 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business contracted from $15.8 million to $10.1 million. I already mentioned higher prices, including those associated with tariffs. But management also mentioned another pain point for the company. They acknowledged, for instance, that they have already started to feel the effects of enforcement from ICE as it cracks down on undocumented workers. Estimates vary, but one source figures that between 15% and 23% of all construction workers in the US are undocumented. Admittedly, management has said that this has not put a big strain on the company as of yet. But it has made its effects known.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. Because of the weak third quarter, revenue for this period is down. Even though the company saw an increase in the average net revenue per unit from $60,300 to $68,600, the number of units sold dropped sharply from 1,536 to 1,334. Net profits and cash flows fell across the board as well. Despite these problems, a very successful September for its Fort Worth assets indicated that strong demand will exist for the final quarter of 2024 and into the first quarter of 2026. The company also believes that recent acquisitions, such as of AmeriCasa, FutureHomeX, and its Houston lot, will permit between 50% and 60% more retail units next year, maybe even more if things go well, compared to 2025 in its entirety.

For context, management agreed at the end of October to acquire AmeriCasa in a deal valued at $12 million cash as well as certain other consideration in the form of assumed liabilities. Meanwhile, its purchase of FutureHomeX brings into the fold a cloud-based software as a service platform that helps manufactured home retailers and communities. This should certainly draw in some additional revenue. And when it comes to the law in Houston, that purchase could add 100 homes on its own. So at the end of the day, the company remains growth-oriented. And considering that it has no debt on its books and enjoys a cash position of $13.6 million, it certainly has the fuel with which to grow.

If we project our results based on what we have seen already for the year, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. The 2025 trading multiples are based on the assumption that net profits will be $43.9 million, that adjusted operating cash flow will be $45.9 million, and that EBITDA will total $52.1 million. In addition to being cheap on an absolute basis, the stock is also attractively priced compared to other home builders, most of whom don't play in the tiny home and manufactured space. In the table below, you can see precisely what I mean. Across the board, only one of these five enterprises is cheaper than our candidate.

Company Price/Earnings Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Legacy Housing Corporation 10.7 10.2 8.8
Hovnanian Enterprises (HOV) 6.5 6.7 8.2
Beazer Homes USA (BZH) 15.0 21.6 27.3
LGI Homes (LGIH) 11.6 117.7 26.3
Smith-Midland Corporation (SMID) 15.4 19.3 15.4
Cavco Industries (CVCO) 25.0 23.4 17.8

Takeaway

Fundamentally speaking, I know that the recent data provided by management was perhaps rather jarring. It certainly throws into question just how strong my previous assumption was regarding how resilient this industry would be compared to the traditional home building market. Having said that, management remains focused on future growth. Certain issues might affect bottom-line results, such as those involving tariffs, high-interest rates, immigration enforcement, and more. But given how solid the company is from a fundamental standpoint, I don't mind maintaining it as a "Buy" candidate.

data.title: Sensata Technologies: Improvements, Albeit Very Modest
data.description:
A little over a year ago, I concluded that Sensata Technologies Holding plc (ST) was a sensor play that was awaiting some green shoots. Despite a promising position, investors were left disappointed multiple times in its past, with reported sales trending flattish and earnings being highly adjusted.

Leadership changed as the business divested or halted some underperforming activities, offering some potential, yet the proof of burden was really with the new management team.

Ever since, some small green shoots are seen, yet in between reported sales declines, flattish earnings, and tiny improvements in organic growth and leverage, I am still cautious to get too upbeat here.

All About Sensing

Sensata is based on the Latin word "sensate," standing for those gifted with sense. With over 100 years of experience in mission-critical and sensor-rich solutions, the company helps customers with a wide range of such technologies in many environments. Over 18,000 employees help its clients to make safer, cleaner, more efficient, and electrified products.

The company actually was part of Texas Instruments following the second World War until private equity firm Bain bought the business in the 2000s, to bring it public in 2010.

Over the past decade, the company has seen quite a mixed performance as revenues rose from $3 billion a decade ago to peak at $4 billion in 2022/2023, sales which ever since have come down a bit. In between very modest dividends and share buyback programs, the track record is far from impressive here. This has been causing an overhang on the shares for the right reasons and for years now.

Perspective

Early in 2024, Sensata posted its 2023 revenues at $4.05 billion, pretty flat compared to 2022, although that organic revenue growth was reported at 1.5%. The company reported adjusted earnings of $3.64 per share, and while this was a substantial number (in relation to the share price), GAAP losses were reported amidst heavy adjustments made to earnings.

Following a soft start to 2024, CEO Jeff Cote stepped down in the spring, lifting some hopes for change, badly needed amidst the lackluster topline and earnings trends. Moreover, net debt of $2.89 billion was substantial, translating into a 3.2 times leverage ratio based on EBITDA of $902 million.

Furthermore, new management initiated an SKU rationalization program, which would hurt top-line sales but could be meaningful to margins, as such moves might really initiate some improvements, albeit far from a given.

What Happened?

Over the past year and a bit, shares of Sensata have largely traded around the $30 mark, trading far below that during the tariff turmoil in the spring, now having recovered to current highs at $36, marking modest gains since my last take in September of last year.

In part driven by a strong dollar (at the time) and rationalization of SKUs, the company reported a 3% fall in 2024 sales to $3.93 billion. The company posted GAAP earnings of $0.85 per share, with adjusted earnings falling a bit to $3.44 per share; these numbers are again rather adjusted. The company quantified the SKU rationalization impact at $300 million, guiding for 2025 sales at $3.6 billion, although this should hopefully translate into better margins.

The company furthermore made some progress with net debt, which fell to $2.63 billion, and as EBITDA fell a bit to $882 million, leverage ratios were down a bit to 3.0 times.

In May, Sensata reported a 9.5% fall in first quarter sales to $911 million, with adjusted earnings down eleven cents to $0.78 per share. With net debt being flattish and EBITDA under further pressure, leverage ratios actually ticked up a bit. By July, second-quarter sales were reported down 8.9% to $943 million, driven by the same rationalization efforts; however, this no longer weighed to the same extent on earnings, with earnings down five cents to $0.87 per share.

By October, third-quarter sales were reported down 5.2% to $932 million, with smaller revenue declines aided by an annualization of US rationalization efforts, a softer dollar, as organic growth improved to 3.2%, although that organic growth for the first nine months of the year came in at a disappointing -1.0%. Adjusted earnings of $0.89 per share were dead flat compared to last year; however, not all was good, as the company took a $225 million goodwill impairment charge on its Dynapower business due to reversal of green energy policies.

Net debt came down further to $2.43 billion, and with EBITDA down to $829 million on a trailing basis, relative leverage ratios remain at the higher end at 2.9 times, although trending downwards. For the fourth quarter, the company saw sales of around $905 million, with adjusted earnings seen at a midpoint of $0.85 per share.

And Now?

Note that Sensata remains a buyer of its shares here, after $120 million was spent on share buybacks this year, with some 146 million shares outstanding right now. Trading at $36, the market value stands at $5.2 billion, for a $7.6 billion enterprise valuation. This values the business at around 2 times sales, about 9 times EBITDA, and around 10 times adjusted earnings; these earnings are still quite adjusted.

Frankly, I am a bit in doubt. Valuations remain reasonable, but growth is not convincing. On the bright side, following an absolute decline in leverage and the modest advancements on a relative basis, some organic growth is seen. However, the year-to-date these remain very soft, certainly given the promise of SKU rationalization and improved focus.

Moreover, I am mindful that shares have seen quite a rally recently, still trading at just $28 in November. While some improvements are seen under the hood, I am not too impressed, as the recent 25% rally in the time frame of a couple of weeks has extended shares a bit again.

For now, I am waiting for more tangible proof, and while adjusted metrics remain very modest, they remain quite adjusted (albeit a bit less with the passage of time) as leverage gradually, but really gradually, is coming down.

data.title: S&P 500 Bears: Stop Fighting The Last War
data.description:
SP500 and its warning signs

These days, with stock indices (such as the DJI, SP500, NASDAQ, and RTY) all hovering around record highs across the board, it has almost become a daily routine for me to see at least 1 “warning sign” pushed to my email box. Some recent examples include the high P/E ratios, high Buffett index, high rule of 20 scores, the AI bubble, and also the high concentration in mega-cap stocks (with the MAG 7 stocks being the most notable examples).

With this background, the goal of this article is to explain why this wisdom, much of which is the result of historical statistics, can be outdated and very misleading. Making investment decisions based on them is essentially like fighting the last war. The article will become quite lengthy if I cover all these warnings and explain why their applicability is questionable under today’s market conditions. Plus, I’ve written recently to share my thoughts on some of them already. For example, you can see a recent article titled “S&P 500: Rule Of 20 Likely To Remain Misleading In 2026” with my detailed thoughts on the SP500’s rule of 20 score. Hence, this article will concentrate on the SP500’s (and other indices too, such as the Nasdaq or RTY) high concentration in mega-cap stocks. Discussion of this issue will also involve my thoughts on some related issues, such as the SP500’s valuation and the AI bubble. So hopefully, I will end up with a good balance between depth and breadth.

A particular reason I picked this issue as an entry point to this analysis is that the evidence seemed almost self-evident and irrefutable. As a simple example, the chart below displays the total MC (market cap) and also their weight in the S&P 500 index since the 2000s. As seen, their weight (shown by the red line) has dramatically increased since around 2015. Before that, their collective weight stayed below 16%. But after that, their weight has been on an overall upward trend—a quite sharp one, I might add - and has reached around 40% at present. It of course can make investors feel uncomfortable to come to the recognition that 10 companies demand about 40% of the largest 500 companies in the country. The fact that 9 out of these 10 companies (the only exception is Berkshire Hathaway, BRK) all have common and substantial exposure to the AI industry only adds to the concern.

Next, I will provide my perspective to explain why such seemingly self-evident and irrefutable data are actually refutable.

SP500 profitability without the mega 7

With the equity market’s concentration in a few mega-caps, all with AI exposure, bears love to compare the current market conditions to the 2000s’ dot-com bubble. However, I think these comparisons (at least, the ones I’ve read) missed some of the most fundamental differences.

To start, today’s mega-caps enjoy far better profitability metrics than those during the dot-com bubble. The real danger in investing is not to bid up the price of businesses of good quality but to bid up the prices of businesses with weak profits or even no profits—which was what happened during the dot-com bubble. Today’s mega-caps not only enjoy far superior profitability metrics, far better scalability, and global market reach than the broader market average. Yet, their valuations are not necessarily more expensive than the overall market.

As an example, the chart below compares the profitability metrics of the Magnificent Seven stocks (Mag 7) to the SP500 index. As seen, these stocks generally exhibit far superior profit margins compared to the S&P 500's average net margin of 11.1%, with the exception of TSLA and AMZN. NVIDIA (NVDA), the stock with the largest MC in the index, stands out with the highest margins across the board, including a dominant 70%+ Gross Profit Margin and a remarkable 53%+ Net Income Margin, almost five-fold of the SP500 average. Other companies in this group (such as META, GOOG, APPL, MSFT, etc.) all boast strong net margins, on average threefold better than the SP500 average. Even the lower margins of Tesla (TSLA) at 5.31% and Amazon (AMZN) at 11.06% are a reflection of their unique business models in my view, not their earnings capabilities.

And to put an even broader context to this discussion, the current profitability of the SP500 itself is far better than that during the 2000s—actually far better than any time since the 1970s, according to the statistics shown in the next chart, as seen.

SP500 valuation without the mega-caps

Despite the SP500’s continuously improving profitability and the mega-cap’s even better margins, the mega-caps and overachievers are not necessarily pricier than the overall market. To illustrate this point, the table below compares a popular (and also the largest) market-weighted S&P 500 ETF fund, VOO, to an equal-weighted S&P 500 ETF fund (RSP).

Due to RSP’s equal weight indexing method, the weight of each constituent stock is on average 0.2% (roughly 1/500). Thus, the total weight of the top 10 mega-caps will be around 2% only. As a result, RSP can provide good insights into the valuation differences between the rest of the SP500 and the top mega-cap stocks.

At a global level, VOO’s P/E currently stands around 28.9x. It is indeed higher than RSP’s 24.5x, but the P/E premium is quite mild in my view at 18% when contextualized by the vastly better profitability metrics of the mega-caps as just mentioned. Furthermore, other valuation metrics (such as P/B ratio) even show that VOO is priced at a discount relative to the equal-weighted RSP (5.2x vs. 6.34x). At a tree’s levels, as shown in the second chart below, the mega 7 stocks’ average P/E ratio is not that alarming at all compared to the SP500. If we exclude TSLA, the rest of the mega 7 now trades at a median P/E of around ~31x, not that different from the ~29x P/E of VOO despite their far better profit margins and also growth potential.

Other risks and final thoughts

Another fundamental difference between our current situation and the 2000s’ dot-com bubble is interest rates. As seen in the next chart below, the 2000s saw the highest interest rates in the past 3 decades. Even short-term treasury rates (3-month treasury rates) hovered above 5% for years. Looking ahead, I see little chance for interest rates to be that high for that long again. My detailed thought process behind this prediction can be found in an earlier article; the gist is quoted below:

The debt of the country has expanded many drastically in the past 3~4 decades. In the meantime, corporate tax rates have been drastically lowered over the years per data from the Peterson Foundation. Due to the lower tax rates, the United States now collects fewer revenues from corporations, measured as a percentage of its GDP, than most similarly developed countries. In 2022, for example, the latest year for which data is available for international comparison, U.S. corporate tax revenues accounted for just 1.3 percent of GDP, while the average fraction is 3.2% among the 13 developed countries.

Risk-free rates act as the gravity on all asset valuation, especially equity valuation. A structurally lower risk-free rate should be able to support structurally higher equity valuation.

Before closing, my above analyses are oriented towards the long-term and also at a microscopic level. Near-term issues, such as the ongoing trade disputes, inflation, and geopolitical events, can all impact the equity market’s earnings, profit margins, and also valuation multiples. They can also all impact the risk-free interest rates and cause them to go in the opposite direction as I anticipated above. However, if/when these deviations occur, I consider them to be temporary only. And to be completely fair, most of the traditional wisdom the bears love to quote against the current market (Buffet index, rule of 20, high CAPE ratio, etc.) are all long-term oriented and have no near-term predictive power either.

Plus, these traditional wisdoms are all derived from historical data gathered under macroeconomic and geopolitical conditions that are very different from today’s conditions. In particular, this article offered a few counterarguments against the market’s concentration on a few mega-cap stocks and bubble risk (which many bears love to draw similarities from the dot-com). To me, these arguments missed some of the most fundamental aspects, such as the profitability metrics of the mega-caps, their valuation ratios relative to the index average, and also the role of risk-free interest rates

data.title: BridgeBio Oncology Therapeutics: Promising KRAS Drug Developer - In My View, At Least
data.description:
Investment Overview

This is my first time initiating coverage of BridgeBio Oncology Therapeutics (BBOT), and the first time the company has been covered for Seeking Alpha.

BBOT was initially formed as a subsidiary of BridgeBio (BBIO), a company I first covered for Seeking Alpha in 2023, with a Buy Rating - shares have risen in value by >520% since, on account of the company successfully securing approval for its heart disease drug Attruby (acoramidis) in November last year - the drug has peak revenue expectations of ~$2bn per annum.

Anyway, back to BBOT, which refers to itself as "a clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company advancing a next-generation pipeline of novel small molecule therapeutics targeting RAS and PI3Ka malignancies".

BBOT listed on the Nasdaq in August, after entering into a definitive business combination agreement with Helix Acquisition Corp. II, a special purpose acquisition company ("SPAC") sponsored by affiliates of Cormorant Asset Management, in February this year. Shares began trading priced at $10, and presently trade at $12.5.

As we can see in the slide above (taken from a BBOT corporate presentation), BBOT wants to address two well validated targets in oncology - KRAS and PI3Ka - and improve on the best efforts of other pharmas to date, by bringing next-generation drug candidates to market.

Piqray, a breast cancer med marketed and sold by Swiss Pharma Novartis (NVS), earning >$400m of revenues year-to-date in 2025, is an example of an approved PIK3a inhibiting drug, while Lumakras (sotorasib) and Krazati (adagrasib), marketed and sold by Amgen (AMGN) and Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY) respectively, are KRAS inhibiting lung cancer therapies that have earned a combined ~$325m year-to-date.

BBOT's thesis is that the true promise of targeting KRAS and PI3Ka is yet to be fully exploited, primarily because, as the company notes

  • Approved KRAS inhibitors only target the OFF state which can limit target coverage and durability of response
  • Kinase inhibitors of PI3Ka have modest therapeutic index and safety profiles include restrictions based on glucose levels

All three of its pipeline candidates will read out Phase 1 data in 2026, management says, so this strikes me as a good time to share some thoughts.

BB0-8520 - On / Off Inhibition

KRAS, the protein is apparently either in an active, or an inactive state, or either "OFF", or "ON", and BBOT says that current approved therapies only target the OFF state. According to an abstract I came across on Sciencedirect.com:

Among cancer-driving genes, few are as prominent as KRAS. First identified in a murine sarcoma virus in 1982, the gene is now known to be involved in 14.3% of human cancers. The KRAS protein is a small membrane-bound GTPase important for multiple cell signaling functions.

It exists in two states. When bound to GDP, it is ‘off’. When GDP is exchanged for GTP (usually in response to various growth stimuli), KRAS is turned on, activating the kinases RAF and PI3K and downstream signaling to promote cell proliferation and survival. KRAS returns to the off state when GTP is hydrolyzed to GDP often via GTPase-activating proteins.

BBOT's thesis is that dual ON/OFF inhibition can create sustained pathway inhibition "even after systemic drug levels decline", and that dual inhibition "may prevent adaptive mechanisms of resistance", leading to a favourable safety and efficacy profile.

Of course, this thesis has to be proven in the clinic, and BBOT is running study ONKRAS-101, a Phase 1a dose escalation study (lower dosing is another potential advantage of dual inhibition), evaluating candidate BB0-8520 both as a monotherapy, and in combo with Merck's (MRK) globally best-selling cancer drug, the checkpoint (PD-1) inhibitor Keytruda (pembrolizumab).

In its Q3 earnings press release BBOT states that:

  • Early data from Phase 1 dose escalation showed a 60% confirmed overall response rate in KRAS G12C NSCLC patients.
  • The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (“FDA”) has granted Fast Track designation to BBO-8520 for the treatment of adult patients with previously treated, KRAS G12C-mutated metastatic NSCLC.

As we can see above, in its corporate presentation, BBOT quotes an ORR of 80% (8/10 patients), and the responses appear impressive in terms of tumor shrinkage - from a safety perspective, no adverse events Grade 2 or higher have been observed, although we should note, it is still early in the study, and only ten patients have been enrolled.

BBO-10203 / BBO-11818

This is an orally available medicine, like BBO-8520, designed to "inhibit the physical interaction between RAS and PI3Ka, disrupting signalling in the process. More detail is provided below:

Again, the next step for BBOT is to share some proof-of-concept ("PoC") data - preclinical data has apparently shown "strong activity" in combo with standards of care cancer therapeutics, including trastuzumab - AstraZeneca's (AZN) Enhertu, fulvestrant - the same Pharma's Faslodex, and palbociclib - Pfizer's (PFE) Ibrance - $4.4bn of revenues in 2024.

A Phase 1a study is underway for patients with HER2+ breast cancer, HR+/HER2- breast cancer, KRAS mutant colorectal cancer, and KRAS mutant non-small cell lung cancer, and Phase 1 data should be available in the first half of next year, management says.

Meanwhile, the final candidate in BBOT's pipeline, BBO-11818, is "a panKRAS ON/OFF inhibitor designed with strong potency against G12D and G12V mutants. BBOT says that:

BBO-11818 has selectivity over HRAS and NRAS with the goal of achieving high levels of KRAS inhibition in human tumors. In addition, it has combination potential with BBO-10203 to mitigate the PI3Kα resistance pathway.

The ongoing Phase 1 KONQUER-101 (NCT06917079) trial is evaluating BBO-11818 for patients with locally advanced or metastatic KRAS mutant solid tumors.

Analysis - Ways To Think About Valuation While We Wait For Data

At this stage it is very difficult to assign value to BBOT's pipeline assets as positive clinical data trumps positive preclinical data every time (if that were not the case, the failure rate of drug candidates would not be so high - no biotech enters the clinic with a drug that underperformed preclinically).

The early Phase 1 data for BBO-8520 does strike me as impressive however - despite being a highly prized target, neither Krazati not Lumakras ever delivered a truly outstanding set of clinical data.

Krazati achieved ORR of 34% in its pivotal study in colorectal cancer, while Lumakras recorded an ORR of 26%, and did not meet its overall survival endpoint in its CRC pivotal study, and an ORR of 36% in its pivotal NSCLC study), which led to the FDA denying the drug a full approval in 2023.

The jury is still out on KRAS, then, both in terms of its effectiveness as a target, and its commercial viability - sales of Krazati and Lumkras have fallen well short of expectations, although their best days may be ahead.

BBOT's market cap valuation is almost exactly $1bn - I would say that is high for a Phase 1 stage biotech, although value also comes from an experienced management team - CEO Eli Wallace discovered and developed first-in-class HIF2α inhibitor Welireg - indicated for kidney cancer and marketed and sold by Merck (MRK), earning revenues of $495m year-to-date in 2025 - while at Peloton Therapeutics (acquired by Merck for $2.2bn in 2019), and was also involved in development of Mektovi and AstraZeneca's Koselugo, which earned $630m of revenues last year.

Chief Science Officer Pedro Beltan was formerly a Director of Oncology Research at Amgen (AMGN), while Chief Medical and Science Officer Yong (Ben) Ben has a background in venture capital, as well as roles at AstraZeneca (AZN), and multiple biotechs.

Whether the success of parent BridgeBio should be considered as value enhancing for BBOT is open to debate, but it certainly doesn't harm BBOTs prospects that its leadership team have been involved in multiple drug approvals over a period of decades.

BBOT reported $468m of cash as of the end of September 2025, and a year-to date net loss of $(102.4m), compared to $(59m) in the prior year period. Drug development is an expensive business, and doubtless BBOT will look to raise funding on numerous occasions over the years, which investors should be prepared for.

Concluding Thoughts - What Is The Call On BBOT Today - Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As mentioned, with programs only recently entered into the clinic, any call on BBOT is going to be fairly objective and based on gut feel to a certain extent.

It's fair to say that I have seen much lower valuation biotechs - think $200 - $300m market cap - whose pipelines are further advanced and objectively more promising than BBOT's, but should we entertain the thesis that the senior management "brains trust", with the luxury of being to able to develop new assets in areas they know intimately, from the ground up, will deliver some ground-breaking data and therapies?

I followed the development of Krazati and Lumakras quite closely (back when Krazati was being developed by Mirati Therapeutics, before its acquisition by BMY), and I must say, I feel as though there is "unfinished business" in the KRAS space, and therefore consider BBOT's approach to be quite promising.

On the other hand, did Amgen and Mirati do the best they possibly could, and if so, might BBOT's efforts in revisiting the KRAS space be in vain?

Personally, I favour the bull case - there are three major data readouts due next year, and I suspect at least one may show some genuine promise, creating share price upside, and allowing management to raise more funding, with investors compensated for the dilution with a higher overall valuation of their stock.

It won't be easy - every time a biotech makes the headlines with a breakthrough data readout that doubles its stock price, another biotech is likely delisted, or comes up empty handed with a study readout -investors in BBOT should understand they could end up empty-handed.

As such, I'd urge readers to complete their own due diligence into the company before making any decision.

A final thought is that the market opportunity for a successful KRAS targeting drug is not in doubt, given how many cancers it is over-expressed in. BBOT is playing for high stakes, in my view, and I am fairly sure management will not want to fall at the first hurdle, which sets up some intriguing data catalysts for 2026.

data.title: Iron Mountain: Business Issues And High Valuation Make Me Wary (Downgrade)
data.description:
As I’ve covered in a previous article, Iron Mountain Incorporated (IRM) has made some efforts to improve its business profile in recent years and has pushed for growth in data centers, but this was clearly reflected in its premium valuation at the time. Not surprisingly, its shares have performed quite badly since then, being down by about 19% when including dividends and underperforming the overall stock market during the same period by a wide margin, as can be seen in the next graph.

This poor performance has been justified, in my opinion, by its too high valuation that didn’t make any sense compared to its own history and its peers, plus there has been recently a "short seller" report from Gotham City Research that has raised some concerns and led to further negative investor sentiment toward Iron Mountain in recent weeks.

From an operating perspective, Iron Mountain has been in a restructuring mode over the past few years (project Matterhorn) that is expected to be finished by the end of 2025. In this restructuring program, the company has pushed for growth on technology-driven solutions, particularly related to data management and digital transformation solutions for its customers aiming to better prepare the company for technological developments globally and shift away its business from its more "traditional" records management segment.

This strategy has delivered good results over the past few years, as Iron Mountain’s revenue has grown at double-digit rates over the past four years. The company expects to report annual revenues of about $6.9 billion in 2025, being well on track to achieve its goal set a few years ago of reaching revenues of around $7.3 billion by 2026.

Its revenues are expected to grow at a double-digit rate in 2025 and only need to increase by around 6% YoY during 2026 to reach its target, which seems to be easily achievable. This clearly shows that Iron Mountain’s execution of its business strategy has been positive in recent years, with the trust not expecting to change much its strategy when the Matterhorn project ends at the end of 2025.

This means Iron Mountain is expected to maintain its focus on shifting its business toward technological-driven solutions and allocate most of its capital expenditures (capex) toward data centers, like it has done in the recent past. Beyond data centers, the company is also investing in digital solutions and asset lifecycle management (ALM) services, which together have been its major growth drivers in the recent past and are expected to continue to drive growth ahead. Indeed, these segments represented about 12% of the company’s revenue in 2021, while its combined weight is expected to reach around 28% this year and some 40% of total revenues by 2029.

Other business segments are also increasing at high-single digits, which is still a positive outcome, but due to much stronger growth in data center, digital solutions, and ALM, its business is expected to gradually shift toward these higher growth segments over the next few years. This means Iron Mountain’s growth prospects are relatively good ahead, as the company is likely to report double-digit revenue growth rates over the next few years.

Regarding its most recent financial performance, Iron Mountain’s revenues amounted to more than $5 billion during the first nine months of 2025 (9M 2025), up by 10.7% YoY. However, due to higher cost of sales, its gross margin declined by 70 basis points (bps) during the period, to 55.4% in 9M 2025.

Its operating expenses amounted to more than $4.2 billion in the first nine months of the year, increasing by 10.3% YoY, leading to an operating income in the period of $823 million (+13% YoY) and showing that Iron Mountain was able to have some operating leverage in recent quarters. As the trust has raised significant debt in recent months, its interest expenses were up by 15.6% YoY to $609 million in 9M 2025, leading to a net income in the period of only $55 million (-27% YoY).

However, like many trusts in the REIT sector, Iron Mountain usually focuses on non-GAAP measures, which in my opinion investors should treat with caution as these metrics can be manipulated by management to some extent. Having said that, its adjusted EBITDA was $1.86 billion in 9M 2025, up by 15% YoY and its adjusted funds from operations (AFFO) amounted to $1.1 billion (+14% YoY).

Reflecting this business growth, the company announced a 10% dividend increase to quarterly distribution of $0.8640 per share, implying an annual dividend of $3.456 per share. Based on AFFO per share guidance of between $5.04-5.13 per share in 2025 this leads to a payout ratio around 68%, which seems to be sustainable over the medium to long term. At its current share price, Iron Mountain is currently offering a forward dividend yield around 4%, which is not among the highest in the REIT sector, but it’s certainly more attractive than when I last analyzed the company, as it was yielding just 2.55% at the time.

Regarding its balance sheet leverage, Iron Mountain’s leverage ratio was 5x based on the trust’s definition at the end of last September, being comfortable within its desired range of 4.5-5.5x. However, investors should note that this ratio seems to be somewhat optimistic, as the company’s net debt was about $15.3 billion at the end of last September, leading to a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 6.54x based on its own adjusted EBITDA.

Additionally, investors should note that Iron Mountain’s adjusted EBITDA metric excludes stock-based compensation and restructuring costs, two items that have been considerably over the past few years. Therefore, using a more conservative approach for EBITDA adding back just depreciation & amortization to operating income, I arrive at an adjusted EBITDA of $1.57 billion in 9M 2025, which is about 16% lower than compared to the company’s adjusted EBITDA figure. Based on this EBITDA value, its current leverage is about 7.8x, being much higher than what the company is reporting to investors.

This shows that it is important to treat non-GAAP metrics with caution, as companies can define it differently across the REIT sector and make comparisons between trusts trickier for investors. Indeed, this was one of Gotham City Research issues with Iron Mountain, as the short seller also calculates leverage at a much higher level than compared to the company’s disclosed metrics. For investors who want to dig deeper into the short sellers’ claims, you can see the full report on this link.

Personally, I have read the report and think Iron Mountain’s adjustments are aggressive and questionable and, as I’ve said many times in previous articles on Medical Properties Trust (MPW) for instance, investors should always treat non-GAAP metrics with caution and calculate important KPIs using different assumptions and do not treat reported non-GAAP metrics as "true."

Another issue with Iron Mountain is its poor free cash flow generation, as the trust’s cash flow from operating activities is not enough to finance its capital expenditures and other cash outflows related to investing activities. As investors can see from the company’s 10-Q report (in the cash flow statement), its cash from operating activities was only $840 million during 9M 2025, while its capex amounted to $1.755 billion and other cash expenses resulted in cash outflows from investing activities of close to $2 billion in the period.

As can be seen in the previous figure, this is something that has been persistent in the past, explaining why net debt has increased significantly over the past few quarters as Iron Mountain has been investing more aggressively in data centers (which account for the majority of capex). Indeed, net debt increased by almost $2.5 billion during the first nine months of 2025, as the company has rollover debt and issued new bonds, to finance its cash burn and also to finance its dividend payments. This is clearly not sustainable over the long term, and from a credit perspective, it’s also not a positive background. Its credit rating is already high yield, showing that its credit profile is relatively weak, while its need to continue to raise debt to finance its cash burn is likely to result in higher interest costs ahead and should put further pressure on its cash flow generation.

This puts the company business model into question as the trust likes to report adjusted figures that show a healthy business, but that profile is not backed by its cash flow generation, and its rising debt levels are clearly concerning.

Moreover, management has not reacted publicly to the Gotham City Research report and has said this in a recent conference:

Yes. So look, that report is highly inaccurate. I know there's been some questions. Let me just say it very clearly, there's no investigations of any sort going on. And of course, we shared the full report with our Board, and they agreed with us that it lacks any merit to even publicly comment on. So that's why we haven't addressed it in a public forum prior to this. And I think about our performance, Brendan, as our team has been delivering outstanding results for several years now. And I'd rather let our performance, both our past and our future performance speak for itself.

Personally, I do think that Gotham City Research raises some valid points about Iron Mountain’s financial disclosures and use of adjusted figures, so this answer was a disappointment and makes me wary about management’s transparency to investors. Iron Mountain’s presentations to investors are largely focused on adjusted figures that show a business that is growing, while the cash burn and higher leverage are not referred to in presentations. I think transparency is key for investors to have trust in management, and Iron Mountain doesn’t seem to be doing a good job regarding this.

Regarding its valuation, this was one of my major concerns about Iron Mountain’s shares in the past, as the trust was trading at 32x Free Funds from Operations (FFO) at the time, which was at a significant premium to peers. As the share price has declined in recent months and FFO has increased in the meantime, Iron Mountain is now trading at around 25x FFO, which still seems to be excessive. However, this is now closer to its peer Equinix (EQIX), but remains above Digital Realty’s (DLR) valuation, plus investors should be aware that data centers are still a relatively small part of Iron Mountain’s business and should, therefore, trade at a discount to its data center peers in my opinion.

Conclusion

Iron Mountain has made good progress on its business restructuring over the past few years, leading to higher revenues and earnings in recent years. However, this has not translated into higher free cash flow and debt levels are rising to relatively high levels, leading to much higher balance sheet leverage than what the company has been reporting based on its own metric.

The recent attack from a short-seller seems to have some merit, but the company has not addressed this in the best way, in my opinion, raising some questions about its financial figures and issues about its business model. Moreover, its valuation remains quite high, which doesn’t seem to be supported by its fundamentals. Given this backdrop, I think Iron Mountain is a stock to avoid and my recommendation changes to Sell.

data.title: UnitedHealth: A Long-Term Compounder Worth Holding Through Any Cycle
data.description:
Following my previous article on UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH), the stock rebounded from undervalued levels and appreciated by 10%. Over roughly the past 100 days, UNH has also outperformed the benchmark by a few percentage points, and was close to my guided $400 price target. It reached $381 on Oct. 28, but then consolidated and currently trades at approximately $337.

The recent outperformance and rebound from a multi-year low appear to be promising. It signals to me that sentiment may be gradually shifting, and this also raises investor confidence. A recent Q3 2025 double-beat performance helped a lot for repricing, underlining a double-digit top-line growth. It could also signal that the majority of the concerns may already be in the rearview mirror.

I can tell already that my bullish thesis remains unchanged. I still think that for long-term investors who favor market dominance at an undervalued valuation, it may be attractive at this current price tag. At a forward P/E of about 19x versus the FY2026 EPS estimate, UNH is discounted to both peers and the benchmark, and that fuels my bullish outlook.

I still rate UnitedHealth a "Buy" with a forecast price target of $400. This reflects about 19% upside potential, which would indicate broader market outperformance. I would also like to emphasize that this estimate also doesn't consider dividend income, and a yield of 2.63% could appear as an appealing bonus to an undervalued healthcare name.

Q3 2025: Double-Beat With Double-Digit Revenue Growth

On Oct. 28, the company delivered Q3 2025 results. Finally, after three consecutive top- or bottom-line misses, the company managed to deliver a double-beat performance. With accelerated revenue growth, it may seem that UNH is prepared to extend its dominance in the space. However, the market reacted to the results with a 3.42% decline over the following trading session.

UnitedHealth posted $113.16 billion in revenue, signaling accelerated 12% growth over the past 12 months, and nearly $104 million above what the market had anticipated. The bottom line arrived at $2.92 in diluted EPS, a 2.4x contraction versus $7.15 in the same quarter last year, but $0.11 above analysts' estimates. Based on the market reaction, I think this could have been a "buy the rumor, sell the news" type of event, highlighting that UNH surged from approximately $250 on Aug. 5 to $380 on the earnings day. This captures a 44% rally over a few months, and it seems natural for profit-taking to occur.

The chart above highlights an interesting correlation between the UNH stock price and top-line performance. Since 2021, it has been closely correlated. Based on the trend, the stock price was following the anticipation of top-line growth. Although revenue expansion hasn't slowed down, at the end of 2024, UNH began its correction and has been trending downward ever since.

The initial inflection point was the unfortunate event tied to the murder of the previous UnitedHealth CEO, Brian Thompson, on Dec. 4, as the stock price dropped 6% over the following trading session. Since then, the stock has collapsed, and the bearish narrative intensified. Pressure from social and broader media with viral stories about significant claim denial rates, elevated scrutiny on PBMs, and other regulatory and legal headwinds stressed the stock. There were obvious reasons behind a dramatic shift in sentiment, but the company's revenue growth remains intact. This presents business stability, resilience, and a strong moat.

Back to the report itself, in the press release, management indicated that it has experienced a drop in both earnings from operations and a margin decline. Although this was anticipated, it reflects approximately 2x contraction to $4.3 billion, and 390 bps deterioration in net margin, highlighting 2.1%. A rise in medical costs remains an ongoing issue, but I think UNH may still turnaround from the current valuation if they subside.

On the upside, UNH achieved an increase in total served customers. It has exceeded 50 million in total, with approximately 800,000 new additions in Q3 2025. The offerings tied to employer and individual experienced an addition as well, highlighting nearly 700,000 additions in self-funded, and a 200,000 increase in domestic clients.

Now, a significant downside is tied to the fact that its Optum brand is also facing bottom-line headwinds. I would like to emphasize that the deterioration in margins is across Optum operations, which are mainly driven by reductions in Medicare funding. Of course, a rise in utilization and costs also had a meaningful impact.

The good thing is that top-line growth remains intact. Although Optum has achieved an 8% revenue growth versus Q3 2024, the core segment of UNH posted more than 16% expansion in top-line, underlining $87 billion over the past three months.

Despite the bottom-line headwinds, the company's cash flow remains strong. In addition to this, the decline in diluted EPS was anticipated due to a rise in medical costs, and the market seems to have already priced this in the stock, once it collapsed to a 52-week low of $234.60. UNH is still a highly profitable healthcare giant, and once sentiment improves, it may continue its uptrend in the stock price, alongside improving fundamentals and performance.

The healthcare sector of the S&P 500 now trades at 18.47, and although it has rebounded from the recent multi-year low of about 16.50x, it has a long way to go for upside. The current environment was risk-on, and that is not favorable for the defensive sector. However, I think it is uplifting that UNH strongly rebounded from yearly lows, and if the volatility returns to the broader markets, it may reach my price target.

UnitedHealth's stock has been following top-line performance for years. The stock entered a downward channel a year ago, but after this meaningful pullback, it appears to have found a bottom and rebounded strongly from the yearly lows. I think that the majority of the headwinds may already be priced in the stock price, and it could gradually return to the correlation trend. I would also like to emphasize that it is impressive that UNH maintained a significant top-line increase despite bottom-line headwinds. Yes, the margins eroded, but it also could be close to the bottom, and the forward-looking markets may hold on to the turnaround story, reflecting an elevated premium, because of UNH's significant stance in the market.

UNH Is Still Cheap At This Price Tag, More Upside Ahead

I kindly invite you to read my previous article on UnitedHealth. It offers a more in-depth analysis of the company's valuation and also analyzes its shareholder-friendly capital allocation approach through dividends. Nevertheless, there are a few things I would like to elaborate on.

UnitedHealth trades at a forward P/E of approximately 21 versus the FY2025 EPS estimate and a 19x earnings multiple versus the FY2026 EPS expectations. I think that the 15-month forward P/E seems to be more reasonable, and I will use it below. But I would also like to flag that the chart above highlights that even a 21x earnings multiple presents a business-typical valuation. This argues that there is room for multiple expansion if steady top- and bottom-line growth continues.

Therefore, at a 19x earnings multiple, the healthcare giant trades approximately in line with peers at 19x. Versus the average company in the S&P 500 at a 23x earnings multiple, UNH remains discounted by 21%. Given the company's well-known brand, significant moat, and stable top-line performance, I think it deserves at least the benchmark's earnings multiple.

To support this, over the past 12 months, UNH achieved 10% revenue growth. This matches the performance of the average company in the S&P 500 at historical 10% revenue growth. Furthermore, UNH delivered about a 25% bottom-line increase on a year-over-year basis, and if we compare to the benchmark again, this presents a 2.5x outperformance versus recent years' 10% bottom-line growth. The concerns tied to a substantial rise in medical costs are real, but I argue that UNH remains undervalued at these levels.

Now, the short-term outlook remains uncertain, and I wouldn't be surprised that until concerns are resolved, UnitedHealth continues trading in a range. However, talks are in progress around the extension of the ACA and its subsidies, and that could heavily contribute to my bullish outlook. This also remains the highest risk for the company in the short term, and investors should monitor the outcome.

Nevertheless, I think UNH is here to stay. Its impressive resilience, highlighting a solid revenue growth despite the headwinds, argues for at least a market-like valuation. I kindly invite you to read my previous article on the company, but to sum up, UnitedHealth is still cheap at the current price tag. If tailwinds play out favorably and ACA extensions are enrolled, I would not be surprised if UNH rebounded from the current trading range.

Wall Street estimates $17.75 in diluted EPS for FY2026, reflecting a resumed bottom-line performance. I believe the company is well-positioned to achieve this target, especially if the bill passes and uncertainty fades away. If that happens, I think that investors may reprice UnitedHealth at a closer-to-market-like valuation. Thus, if we apply approximately a 22.5x earnings multiple, leaving a little bit of margin of error, to the FY2026 EPS estimate of $17.75, we arrive at my price target of $400. Time will tell, but this seems to be a reasonable target if tailwinds materialize.

My Bullish Narrative Remains, UnitedHealth Is A "Strong Buy"

My bullish narrative hasn't changed. UnitedHealth may appear compelling from different perspectives. It's a defensive stock with a dividend income. Also, the whole healthcare industry of the benchmark trades at multi-year lows. Sure, there are reasons behind suppressed valuations, but despite meaningful bottom-line headwinds, UnitedHealth delivers robust and resilient performance, indicating revenue growth.

Main risks and concerns remain, as I have indicated above, and in my previous article. Medical costs and elevated utilization remain the key concerns, but I argue that they have also been the main catalysts behind a sharp correction. The healthcare industry remains sensitive to regulatory changes, and investors should keep in mind that it could also be exposed to volatility. Also, it has a heavily leveraged capital structure, and a hawkish Fed could cloud the outlook. I would also like to emphasize that, due to its defensive origin, it may be less sensitive to macro policy and the Fed's stance, but it's important to highlight that it impacts sentiment.

However, I think UnitedHealth remains an interesting diversification opportunity. It is a market-leading player in the space that also offers a defensive approach. It could help withstand market turbulence, and yield comes as quite a nice bonus to an undervalued name. Thus, my bullish narrative remains unchanged. I still think UnitedHealth is a "Strong Buy." I forecast a $400 price target over the next 12 months, representing a 19% upside potential. In fact, 26 Wall Street analysts forecast a $392 price target, highlighting a 16% upside possibility.

data.title: The Play On ADMA Biologics: A Discounted Stock With Good Prospects
data.description:
I am circling back ADMA Biologics (ADMA) I last looked at this intriguing biotech concern in early August following Q2 results. I am revisiting ADMA Biologics as the company posted Q3 numbers and provided guidance that further confirms that management is building a bright future for shareholders as EPS is projected to quadruple by 2030. Growth that is a rarity in an overbought market. An updated analysis follows below.

Company Overview:

This New Jersey based firm finished its network of 10 plasma collection facilities after several years in 2023. From these inputs, ADMA Biologics produces two FDA approved products, BIVIGAM and ASCENIV. The stock currently trades around $20.00 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of just over $4.8 billion.

Third Quarter Results:

ADMA Biologics posted its Q3 numbers on November 5th. The company delivered GAAP earnings of 15 cents a share. This was in line with the consensus. GAAP net income came in at $36.4 million. This was up just one percent from the same period a year ago. However, this was largely due to a higher effective tax rate. Adjusted EBITDA rose an impressive 29% to $58.7 million.

Revenues rose 12% on a year-over-year basis to $134.2 million. This beat expectations by north of $3 million. Based on results, management upped its FY2025 sales guidance to $510 million from $500 million. FY2026 guidance was bumped to at least $630 million from $625 million. Net income projections rose significantly to $255 million in the coming fiscal year from $240 million.

In addition, ADMA Biologics continues to advance its SG-001 hyperimmune IVIG (hIVIG 10%) program. This candidate is designed to provide passive immunity against Streptococcus pneumoniae in immunocompromised patients. Management believes this could be an eventual $300 million to $500 million high margin opportunity. ADMA Biologics also has a voucher that could accelerate development and approval by two quarters.

The recently FDA approved manufacturing process change will start to be utilized in the fourth quarter. This should improve yields by 20%, which should start to significantly improved margins and profits going forward. In addition, management reaffirmed FY2029 sales guidance of at least $1.1 billion. This projection does not include any contribution from the SG-001 program, it should be noted.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

Just under eight percent of the outstanding float in the shares are currently held short. The CEO of the company has sold approximately $1.7 million worth of shares since July 1st. That said, he still retains the vast majority of his stake in the firm and that has been the only insider activity in the shares in the second half of 2025.

ADMA Biologics ended the third quarter with just over $60 million of cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet against approximately $70 million of long-term debt based on the 10-K the company filed for Q3. The company also has an undrawn on $225 million credit revolver. The company purchased $23 million of its own stock in the third quarter. Since third quarter numbers hit the wires, Cantor Fitzgerald has reissued its Buy rating and $30 price target on ADMA.

Conclusion:

The current analyst firm consensus has the company with GAAP earnings of 59 cents a share on $511 million in sales, up 19% from FY2024. They see profits rising to 91 cents a share in FY2026 on sales growth in the mid-20s.

The shares trade at just over 22 times FY2026E EPS. This a significant discount to the overall market multiple. And few firms in the S&P 500 are projected to more than quadrupled profits by FY2030 and have a likely pathway for doing so.

One month ago, when ADMA was trading at $16.00 a share, the stock was recommended exclusively to Biotech Forum members as a straight equity buy. The stock is up approximately 25% since then. Therefore, I would not add via stock purchases to my current holdings, but would if the shares drifted back to the $16 to $18 a share range.

However, there is decent liquidity and bid/ask spreads in the options against the equity. A covered call strategy can either establish an initial holding in ADMA at a significantly lower entry point or earn a nice return if the stock trades flat or rises over the option duration. And that seems the right play in ADMA stock at current prices.

data.title: RPV: Risks Point To Possible Underperformance
data.description:
The Invesco S&P 500® Pure Value ETF (RPV), launched on 03/01/2006 and managed by Invesco Capital Management LLC, provides exposure to the S&P 500 constituents that exhibit value characteristics such as low P/E, P/B, and P/S ratios. It charges an expense ratio of 0.35% and manages $1.4 billion.

While I see the value in a value approach for long-term investors, the near-term outlook suggests that value is going to underperform growth and even the broad market. RPV's recent outperformance is unlikely to persist for much longer, as the macro conditions will most likely favor different sector allocations.

Portfolio and Performance

RPV's top 10 holdings illustrate the more conservative exposure to large-cap stocks that a value bias results in. The number of holdings is relatively low at 105, but the top 10 account for 27% and the largest weight is only 3.6%.

Consistent with the value approach, Financials have the largest weight at 18.4%, as typical value metrics create a bias toward them even if they're not cheap on an absolute basis. Healthcare, though cheap compared to the index based on conventional metrics, has the advantage of being more conservative than Financials in this macro regime.

The ETF tracks the S&P 500 Pure Value Index, which doesn't just offer another value tilt but filters extreme value within the index and the number of holdings reflects this. The index, and therefore the ETF, is rebalanced on an annual basis, which is long-term enough to allow for the undervalued stocks to mean-revert or get repriced. Taking into account the long time this ETF has been active, the tracking error is negligible, showcasing very high efficiency.

Now, it's certainly interesting that RPV recently caught up to pure-growth stocks, as represented by the Invesco S&P 500 Pure Growth ETF (RPG). However, its YTD performance illustrates something very useful: even during heightened uncertainty regarding the macro conditions, growth stocks led the way.

And that's something that is likely to persist in the near term, considering the present conditions and expectations regarding key macroeconomic variables.

Macro Outlook

Inflation Expectations

Inflation expectations are hovering around 3.2%, which implies further increases from the current CPI and Core CPI levels of 3%.

This is consistent with the idea that tariffs will keep applying some upward price pressure in the short term. The thing is that many businesses might not have exhausted their pre-tariff inventories early this year when there was a surge in imports, and as they do in the near term, inflation will experience a temporary shock. As inflation expectations are partially influenced by past inflation, inflation may actually climb more than current expectations suggest; Gregory Daco, chief economist at EY-Parthenon, anticipated that tariffs should raise inflation by 0.8 percentage points by early next year.

Labor Market Conditions

The labor market is also expected to keep softening, resulting in a "stagflation lite" forecast. YTD, job growth has decelerated meaningfully, unemployment is now up again, and jobless claims have been elevated for some time, pointing to a higher unemployment rate.

Indeed, Global Investment Research estimates that U3 unemployment will climb to 4.5% this year.

Monetary Policy Path

With such an outlook on inflation and the labor market, central bank easing may be bumpy.

The FOMC December meeting concluded on Wednesday with the decision to cut by another 25 bps, the third consecutive cut this year. Also, the Fed announced that it would start to expand its Treasury portfolio on Friday after years of reducing it. However, the last decision was far from unanimous (two members opposed any cut) and Chair Powell didn't indicate any future cuts.

With stocks in the Financials sector being more sensitive to interest rate changes on average, a case for a portfolio with such a meaningful allocation to them is not easy to make. Volatility may, in fact, be higher for value ETFs with not much better returns than the broad market to show for.

This is the key risk here and it's related to opportunity cost. Sure, rates are likely to come down faster now than in the recent past, but due to sticky inflation and much less restrictive rates now, the Fed is more likely to be careful rather than stick to a strong easing narrative. Based on interest rate traders, the probability that we won't get a cut in the next FOMC meeting is now ~75%, consistent with the uncertainty that the Fed wanted to inject.

With inflation not falling rapidly towards the Fed's target and the policy path being so uncertain in the near term, broad value outperformance is not likely; Financials don't benefit in a meaningful way and cyclicals don't experience accelerated demand. Moreover, the undervaluation observed in such stocks should persist, adding another layer of opportunity risk. However, large-cap growth can perform better even during such a regime, as it is often tech-heavy due to secular narratives tied to the current winners, such as cloud and AI adoption.

Growth Trends

The economy is also expected to grow at a slower pace in the near term, at least. Real GDP growth has been quite volatile in 2025, with the first quarter marking a contraction as import growth accelerated, and then it grew by 3.8% in the second quarter, as imports materially declined. The average Real GDP growth in the first half of this year was ~1.4%, a level that, based on economic forecasts, is expected to persist in the next year.

Large-cap Growth ETFs are likely to outperform in this regime, as the largest tech stocks have narratives that make them attractive in a low-growth environment.

Conclusion

As it appears that large-cap value is likely to once again underperform the broader market and growth stocks in the short-term future, I prefer to be cautious about alternative strategies that in other regimes may have been defensive. I am rating RPV a hold for now and will keep monitoring the conditions for any opportunity that suggests a value bias will be useful for portfolios again. It's not a buy because I view growth as more attractive right now, but it's not a sell either because there is no case for it realizing losses in the near- or mid-term.

Now, I am always open to challenging my views because being an ideologue in investing and trading can prove expensive. So if you have a different opinion, this platform is the right place to discuss both strategy and the various options with which we can position our portfolios. Go ahead and leave a comment below to let me know what you think.

Otherwise, please let me know if you found this article useful. It means a lot!

Thank you for reading.

data.title: ARC Resources: Buy The Dip
data.description:
Introduction

We were pretty impressed with Arc Resources, (AETUF) the last time we covered them in mid-2023, giving it a buy in the $14's. The last 2.5 years have been reasonably kind to that call, with the stock grinding higher into mid-June of this year, and a peak of $22.80. It's been downhill until recently as investors were disappointed with reported production from one of their marquee projects that failed to meet expectations. As we've noted in other articles on Canadian-gas focused drillers, ARC shares have suffered by comparison, and under-performed American gas giants as well. Now we understand why.

As we've noted in numerous articles-gas is hot right at the present, encroaching winter time aside, gas has new drivers that didn't exist a few years ago. ARC's is a major Canadian gas producer with 54% of it's daily output of ~360K BOEPD in that commodity.

ARC missed forecasts for EPS and revenue for Q-3 that resulted from some voluntary curtailment's at its Sunrise core production area and a problem with wells their growth area in Attachie. This area has figured prominently in their planning over the last several years and the market was was expecting a robust performance in line with their recent forecast of 30-35K BOEPD. News that Attachie production had fallen short of this forecast by about 8K BOEPD was not well received and put a dent in the stock. The company noted in the release that things were improving at Attachie, raising expectations for Q-4.

October production at Attachie was approximately 29,000 boe per day, including approximately 13,000 barrels per day of condensate. ARC successfully drilled and completed its latest pad applying learnings from earlier in the year, which will be placed on production late in the fourth quarter of 2025.

The company plans significant growth in the next year as demand for gas and condensate-heavy oil diluent, is expected to rise. On a capital budget of $1.8-1.9 bn the company expects to deliver 2026 exit production of 405-420K BOEPD, with free funds flow of $1.5 bn CAD.

Analysts are bullish on the company, giving it a buy rating and with price targets that range from $20-$25.00. The median is $22.00 which would be about a 15% upside from current levels. I think they are being a little timid here as this just gets the company back to second quarter levels. EPS targets of $0.47 for Q-4 are a boost from Q-3's $0.29, and may help propel the stock higher into results are reported on Feb-5th, 2026.

The thesis for ARC Resources

With ARC's Montney position there is room to run for at least couple of decades without any sort of expensive M&A that could damage the balance sheet, or result in dilution to shareholders. This provides predictability for increasing shareholder returns as production ramps up. With a peer leading low cost per mcfe that is in part driven by the quality of the assets held and support processing infrastructure, they can produce profitably across the cycle.

ARC has a solid balance sheet with net debt to FFO just below 2X at current WTI, ~$60.00 and $3.5 NYMEX pricing for gas.

The company is set up to grow its dividend at a sustainable rate, that could accelerate with a declining share count. ARC's shares are priced below intrinsic value at present and the company has been retiring shares as per its NCIB. In Q-3, they bought back 6.5 mm shares and have reduced the share count by 21% over the last four years.

Q-3, 2025 and Guidance

ARC missed estimates in Q-3 substantially, which didn't help its stock as we headed into Q-4. EPS of $0.29 missed by $0.15, and revenue of $799.3 mm missed by $167.26 mm. Projections are for Q-4 EPS of $0.47, and revenues of $1.1 bn. This would be a nice sequential rebound from the Q-3 miss, and a slight shortfall from the same quarter last year. The company hedges about 20% of their 2026 production to preserve cash flow.

The company forecasts production of 405-420 BOEPD in 2026 with capex of $1.8-1.9 bn. This is about a 10% step down from 2025 and the company noted it would deliver higher production and increased free cash from 2025.

Analyzing the Attachie under lift

In the Q&A from the Q-3 call a question was asked specific to the reason for the shortfall from the 30-35K BOEPD forecast to the 25K or so realized. They were a little cagey with the answer-excess water production, but we do have a few data points that will allow me to make an educated guess as to what happened. It's important to understand this as Attachie is one of ARC's growth areas. If the underlift is not fixable with engineering, the overall investment thesis could be compromised.

So here's what we know-

  • Excess water
  • Excess clean up time
  • Refined well design
  • Well spacing

That's actually quite a bit of information, if you have some context in which to frame it. Let's realize that shale strata are laminated often between water-bearing sands. The goal of shale fracking is to position your well bore for maximum drainage once the frac is installed. The risk for well placement is encouraging water production that can leave oil stranded. If you look at the slide below it looks like that Phase I was drilled on a thinner bench than what is projected for Phase II. This amplifies the risk we've discussed in terms of well placement. Maybe the directional guy had a bad day?

One of your challenges as a well designer is to keep the bore hole in the thickest part of the reservoir- to avoid fracking into the water zone, lying just below the shale-gravity, right? Of course. And of course, doing this while also positioning it for maximum drainage-two competing objectives. I think there's a decent chance ARC fracked into water on this pad. This could be the result of drilling out of the zone, an aggressive frac treatment as well as poor well placement. In this scenario part of the well would see higher water cuts than the sections where it was avoided. This is all fixable on future wells with a refined well design, and perhaps finding a new directional guy. As they note in the slide presentation "Use learnings implemented in 2025 and 2026 to drive efficiencies."

Then there is the issue of spacing. With the wine rack or fish bone well pattern, if you don't get this right, then there could be field pressure depletion or "cross-talk" between the wells. This could impact both the cleanup and the production rates that are achieved.

The good news is that all of this is fixable with engineering and not an adverse judgement on the quality of the reservoir.

Risks

Gas prices have been selling off since topping out a few weeks ago. This could chip away at the recent upward momentum ARC stock has seen in the last few weeks. Two things affect gas prices other than the new demand coming from LNG and AI data center investments. Weather and the substitution of coal for power generation. The weather is cooperating-it's chilly out there, but when gas prices spike utilities-that can, switch to coal, and that is probably happening now.

A chilly wind is blowing through the AI buildout "boom" story. Investors are starting to ask, "where's the beef?" (Channeling a tag line from an 80's Wendy's commercial, and worrying that increasing cash flow could go to debt service rather than stock buybacks or dividends.) They worry that increased borrowing to install equipment that may become obsolete before its paid for, leaving the company with an ever-mounting debt profile. Now we are told not to panic. If a bubble is building, it's nothing like the internet bubble of 2000, these companies have real earnings and revenues. Still when the herd stampedes...you don't want to be in the way.

Your takeaway

ARC is trading at an attractive multiple as compared with peers. If you look their flowing barrel number of $27K and give them credit for projected growth, they are slightly more expensive than some. Antero Resources, (AR) trades at $24K per flowing barrel as an example. So it's a bit of a mixed bag on that measure of valuation.

There is no question gas drillers are more expensive than they were six months ago. It would be a little silly for them not to be. Gas has doubled in that time, so there is still some under performance of the equity on that measure.

ARC trades at a ~20% discount on an EV/EBITDA basis to Canadian Natural Resources, (CNQ), and Birchcliff Energy, (BIREF), that's probably induced by the problems we noted earlier. If you gave them a 7.15X multiple shares would rerate toward that $24.00 upper level picked by the analysts. I don't think that's unreasonable with the growth occurring in this market and ARC's plans to increase production.

ARC gets a buy on that basis at current levels.
