data.title: GSK: Depemokimab And Jemperli Lead Next Wave (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Over the past two months, that is how much time has passed since my article "GSK's Hidden Growth Engines Beyond HIV and Oncology," GSK's shares (GSK) (GLAXF) have risen by 12.5%.

And I ask myself: What has aroused investor interest in it? Maybe strong sales of drugs/vaccines or GSK's R&D achievements?

But first of all, I would like to point out that institutional investors have increased their stake in GSK from about 400 million shares to 489.6 million, according to Fintel.

In my view, Wall Street sees that under CEO Emma Walmsley and CMO Sabine Luik, GSK is making successful steps to move beyond being just the leader in the Shingles vaccine market, as well as HIV therapeutics.

By the way, it successfully competes with Gilead's HIV-1 medications (GILD) thanks to Cabenuva [sales reached $474 million in Q3], Dovato [$923 million], and Apretude [$159 million].

It's clear that GSK is targeting, and in my opinion, this is the right strategic decision, three therapeutic areas: oncology, infectious diseases, and respiratory diseases.

So among its experimental drugs, I highlight depemokimab [CRSwNP, asthma, hypereosinophilic syndrome], GSK5764227 [small-cell lung cancer and gastrointestinal cancer], and GSK5458514 [prostate cancer].

But that's not all.

One of GSK's strategies to get closer to its European "friends", Roche Holding (RHHBY) and AstraZeneca (AZN), is to ramp up its M&A activities.

In my view, the key to these is an agreement for up to £268 million with Syndivia to develop an ADC [antibody-drug conjugate] for the treatment of mCRPC. GSK announced it on October 27.

The next day, its investors and I learned of another deal, this time with Empirico, worth up to $745 million.

In it, GSK acquired the worldwide commercial rights to EMP-012, a selective siRNA [small interfering RNA].

I think it's unnecessary to go into the details about the mechanism of action of this class of therapeutic modalities.

I'll just say that siRNA is a molecule that extremely effectively degrades specific mRNA, which ultimately prevents the synthesis of disease-causing proteins.

Empirico doesn't disclose details about the exact gene EMP-012 targets, and in GSK's press release, I only saw three words: "distinct inflammatory pathway."

But I was interested in this product candidate because it's being developed to treat patients with COPD regardless of type 2 status, as well as active smokers.

Yes, EMP-012 is currently only in a Phase 1 study.

But if its preclinical data are confirmed, it would be a severe blow to Sanofi (SNY) and Regeneron (REGN) with their itepekimab, an antibody against IL-33, which, as I mentioned earlier, failed in the AERIFY-2 Phase 3 trial [NCT04751487].

Secondly, and more importantly, GSK will find a successor to Nucala [mepolizumab], which is under pressure in the lung disease therapeutics market from Dupixent.

So, Nucala sales rose by 14.1% year-on-year to $663 million. Meanwhile, Dupixent sales, which I just recently mentioned in my article about Regeneron, reached €4.16 billion in Q3, up 19.6%.

But since I'm interested in more than just the "dry figures", I went further to find out why demand for Dupixent is growing faster than GSK's IL-5 inhibitor. And I found the answer.

SNY/REGN's anti-IL-4/IL-13 monoclonal antibody reduces moderate to severe COPD exacerbations by 30%-34% [Phase 3 BOREAS/NOTUS trials], while Nucala only lowers them by 21% [MATINEE study].

So, I finished with GSK's respiratory and HIV franchises.

And now it's time for Jemperli, which, in my opinion, will make GSK one of the leaders in the oncology market.

Demand for Jemperli is growing faster than I expected

Let me remind you that Jemperli [dostarlimab] is an anti-PD-1 antibody approved by the FDA and EMA as monotherapy and in combination with chemotherapy, carboplatin/paclitaxel, for the treatment of endometrial cancer and dMMR solid tumors.

But before I get into its sales, I wondered about the prevalence of these two types of cancer and their 5-year relative survival rates.

And first, I'll say that endometrial cancer develops from the cells in the lining of the uterus. It accounts for about 95% of all cases of uterine cancer. In turn, the five-year relative survival rate for uterine cancer is 81.1%.

But the situation with the number of cases of endometrial cancer, or as it is called in Latin, corpus uteri, disappointed me even more.

According to the WHO's Global Cancer Observatory, 676,300 women worldwide will be diagnosed with it in 2050, a 60.9% increase over 2022.

And, on dMMR solid tumors, I want to discuss a bit more, as I think not everyone is familiar with it.

These abnormal masses of tissue arise from a defective biological pathway called MMR [DNA mismatch repair].

In turn, dMMR is responsible in our bodies for correcting the DNA replication errors. That is, its task is to prevent the accumulation of mutations, or, if I say more simply, to reduce the likelihood of the formation of cancer cells.

If I look at data from pan-cancer studies, about 2.9% [from 2.7% to 3.1%] of adults with solid tumours have dMMR.

And, if I take endometrial cancer as an example, dMMR prevalence is 25% [22–28%]. For dMMR ovarian cancer, it is lower and amounts to 8% [6-11%].

Well, I have finished explaining what dMMR solid tumors and endometrial cancer are and, as promised, I will move on to discussing the sales of Jemperli.

Despite intensified competition from other PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors, AstraZeneca's Imfinzi and Merck's Keytruda (MRK), sales of Jemperli hit a new all-time high, reaching $303 million in Q3 and beating even my 'best case' scenario by $24 million.

By the way, CCO Luke Miels strengthened my belief that GSK's PD-1 inhibitor continues to occupy a leading position in the endometrial cancer market.

Moving to our growing oncology portfolio, which is up 39%. Jemperli sales were up for the 10th quarter in a row as our teams continue to differentiate Jemperli from the competition, as the only immuno-oncology medicine to demonstrate overall survival in endometrial cancer.

Jemperli's global market share in endometrial cancer is now higher than the leading competitor in DMMR.

But I want to gather more information about Jemperli.

So, according to GSK's Form 20-F, dostarlimab's U.S. and EU patents are set to expire in 2035 and 2036, respectively.

That is, I see this as a greater advantage for its investors since, currently, there are no risks of launching biosimilars to Jemperli for at least the next 10 years.

While its peak sales are expected to exceed £2 billion, CEO Emma Walmsley said in the Q2 call.

We're doing this by focusing strongly on the delivery of the 14 scale opportunities we've previously highlighted, all of them with peak-year sales above GBP 2 billion.

By the way, GSK, realizing its potential in the MSI-H/dMMR market, is actively developing it for the treatment of colon cancer [NCT05855200], head and neck squamous cell carcinoma [NCT06256588, JADE], and rectal cancer [NCT05723562, AZUR-1].

SCO Tony Wood stated on the Q3 earnings call that GSK will announce the results of these three studies between H2 2026 and 2028.

We're following that up with a study called DOMENICA, which is looking at evaluating gemperirdine a chemo-free regimen.

Importantly, as well, obviously, the rectal studies continue to progress well, where we have fantastic complete responses.

Just a quick reminder on some of those programs for you, AZUR-1, which is the locally advanced MSI-H rectal results, which we're expecting to read out in the second half of '26. AZUR-2, which is colon cancer, and there's an interim for that in '28 and the JADE study, which is in the unresectable head and neck setting for which we're also expecting readouts in '28.

So lots of momentum going around Jemperli to continue to support the growth of that medicine.

But my greatest hopes, of course, are for the use of Jemperli for the treatment of dMMR locally advanced rectal cancer.

Why? Due to the outstanding results of the Phase 2 study [NCT04165772] conducted jointly with Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center.

So, 42 patients in the dostarlimab group achieved a 100% clinical complete response rate. If this efficacy is confirmed in the AZUR-1 study, I believe Jemperli will change the treatment paradigm for dMMR/MSI-H rectal cancer.

Risks

Now, it's time to look at my table for risks.

These include RFK Jr./President Trump's information pressure on Big Pharma and weak performance of Zejula [sales of $182 million in Q3 2025 compared to $202 million in Q2] amid increased competition with Pfizer's Talzenna (PFE) and AZN's Lynparza.

Takeaway

Well, I have come to the end of the article. And I didn't forget to discuss how strong or weak GSK's numbers were in Q3.

Its revenue rose 8.6% from $10.39 billion in Q3 2024 to $11.28 billion, beating the consensus estimate by $293.4 million and my 'base case' scenario by $210 million.

GSK's non-GAAP EPS was $1.46, up 13.2% year-over-year, topping Wall Street's expectations by 19 cents or 15.4%.

Thus, Q3 showed me that GSK is thriving.

And with Blenrep [belantamab mafodotin], approved by the FDA on October 23 for the 3L+ treatment for R/R multiple myeloma, GSK's risk/benefit ratio has become even more attractive.
